title: STANDARD FOR CANNED CRAB MEAT CXS 90-1981 Adopted in 1981. Revised in 1995. Amended in 2011, 2013, 2016, 2018.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR CANNED CRAB MEAT CXS 90-1981 Adopted in 1981. Revised in 1995. Amended in 2011, 2013, 2016, 2018.
sections.summary: Document title and status: the Standard for Canned Crab Meat (CXS 90-1981), showing adoption in 1981 and revisions/amendments in 1995, 2011, 2013, 2016 and 2018 (document heading).
Scope: applies to canned crab meat and excludes specialty products in which crab meat constitutes less than 50% m/m of the contents.

Description: defines canned crab meat as meat from leg, claw, body and shoulder (shell removed) of edible Brachyura species and Lithodidae, requires hermetically sealed packing and processing to ensure commercial sterility, and allows any presentation provided it meets the standard and is adequately labelled to avoid consumer confusion.
Product definition: canned crab meat is prepared from leg, claw, body and shoulder meat (shell removed) of edible species of the sub-order Brachyura and all species of the family Lithodidae.
Canned crab meat packed in hermetically sealed containers must receive a processing treatment sufficient to ensure commercial sterility.
Any presentation of the product is permitted provided it meets all requirements of this Standard and is adequately described on the label to avoid confusing or misleading the consumer.

Essential composition and quality: canned crab meat must be prepared from specified live crab suitable for human consumption; packing medium and other ingredients must be food-grade and comply with applicable Codex standards; final product conformity is determined by lot examination per Section 9 using the methods in Section 7 against the defect criteria in Section 8.

Canned crab meat shall be prepared from sound individuals of the species designated in 2.1 that are alive immediately prior to commencement of processing and of a quality suitable for human consumption.
The packing medium and all other ingredients must be food-grade and conform to all applicable Codex standards.
Products comply with this Standard when lots examined in accordance with Section 9 meet the provisions set out in Section 8 and are examined by the methods specified in Section 7.

Permitted additives: acidity regulators and sequestrants listed in Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) for food category 09.4, and only certain Table 3 acidity regulators and flavour enhancers as indicated in that General Standard, are acceptable for use.

Hygiene: products should be prepared and handled in accordance with the General Principles of Food Hygiene and relevant Codex Codes of Hygienic Practice (e.g., CXC 1-1969, CXC 52-2003, CXC 23-1979) and must comply with any microbiological criteria established under CXG 21-1997.

Non-substantive heading indicating that additional specific labelling provisions apply beyond the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods, with no specific requirements listed in this section.
The name of the product shall be either "crab" or "crabmeat."
Non-substantive heading introducing the sampling, examination and analyses requirements for the product.

Sampling: lots for final product examination must be sampled according to an appropriate sampling plan with an AQL of 6.5, and sampling for net weight and drained weight shall follow an appropriate sampling plan established by the CAC.

Samples for sensory and physical examination must be assessed by personnel trained in such examination and tested in accordance with Annex A and the Guidelines for the Sensory Evaluation of Fish and Shellfish in Laboratories (CXG 31-1999).

Net weight determination: weigh the unopened container, remove contents, weigh the empty container (including end and any wrapping after removing excess liquid and adhering meat) and subtract the empty container weight from the unopened container weight to obtain net content.

Drained weight determination: condition containers at 20–30°C for at least 12 hours, pour contents onto a pre‑weighed circular sieve with 2.8 mm square mesh inclined about 17–20° to drain for two minutes, weigh the sieve with drained meat and subtract the sieve mass to obtain drained weight.
Non-substantive heading stating that a sample unit will be considered defective when it exhibits any of the properties defined in the following subsections.

Foreign matter: the presence of any non-crab material that does not threaten human health but is readily recognizable without magnification, or present at a level (including as detected by magnification) indicating non-compliance with good manufacturing and sanitation practices, renders the sample defective.
Odour/flavour defect: a sample unit exhibiting persistent and distinct objectionable odours or flavours indicative of decomposition or rancidity is defective.
Texture defect: excessively mushy or excessively tough flesh uncharacteristic of the species in the presentation constitutes a defect.

Discolouration defect: distinct discolourations indicative of decomposition or rancidity, blue/brown/black discolourations exceeding 5% by weight of the drained contents, or black sulphide staining exceeding 5% by weight of the drained contents render the sample defective.
Defines objectionable matter for the product as any struvite crystal greater than 5 mm in length found in a sample unit.

Specifies lot acceptance criteria: (i) total defectives per Section 8 must not exceed the acceptance number (c) of a sampling plan at AQL 6.5; (ii) total sample units not meeting the form of presentation in Section 2.3 must not exceed acceptance number (c) at AQL 6.5; and (iii) the average net weight and average drained weight (where applicable) of all sample units must be at least the declared weight with no unreasonable shortage in any individual container.

Outlines sensory and physical examination procedures: inspect external can for integrity and distorted ends, open cans and determine weights per Sections 7.3/7.4, examine product for discoloration and foreign/objectionable matter, and assess odour, flavour and texture following CXG 31-1999.
